content.body:
Density: ρ = m/V
ρ = density (kg/m³ or g/cm³)
m = mass (kg or g)
V = volume (m³ or cm³)

Density of water = 1000 kg/m³ = 1 g/cm³
Density of air ≈ 1.2 kg/m³

Relative density (RD) = density of substance / density of water
RD has no units
RD > 1: substance sinks in water
RD < 1: substance floats in water

content.title: Finding Volume by Displacement
content.body:
Irregularly shaped object:
1. Fill measuring cylinder with water, record volume V₁
2. Submerge object, record new volume V₂
3. Volume of object = V₂ - V₁

Example: mass=80g, V₁=50cm³, V₂=70cm³
Volume = 20cm³
Density = 80/20 = 4 g/cm³

content.title: Density Examples
content.body:
Aluminium: 2700 kg/m³ (2.7 g/cm³)
Ice: 917 kg/m³ (less than water → floats)
Oil: ~900 kg/m³ (floats on water)
Lead: 11,340 kg/m³ (sinks fast)
Gold: 19,300 kg/m³

questions.question: Density formula is
questions.explanation: Density = mass ÷ volume

questions.question: Mass 200g, volume 40cm³. Density =
Object with RD=0.8 will
questions.explanation: RD<1 means less dense than water → object floats

questions.question: Density of water is
questions.explanation: Water density = 1000 kg/m³ = 1 g/cm³ at 4°C

questions.question: Ice floats on water because
options: ice density (917 kg/m³) is less than water (1000 kg/m³)
questions.answer: ice density (917 kg/m³) is less than water (1000 kg/m³)
questions.explanation: Ice is ~9% less dense than liquid water — key to aquatic life surviving winter

questions.question: Volume of object: water rises from 25cm³ to 37cm³ when object submerged. Volume =
questions.explanation: Volume = 37-25 = 12cm³ (Archimedes' displacement method)

questions.question: Object mass 150g, volume 50cm³. Will it float in water?
questions.explanation: ρ=3g/cm³ > 1g/cm³ (water) → sinks

questions.question: Units of density in SI system
questions.explanation: SI unit of density is kg/m³ (though g/cm³ is commonly used in chemistry/everyday)

questions.question: Oil floats on water because
options: oil density (~900 kg/m³) is less than water (1000 kg/m³)
questions.answer: oil density (~900 kg/m³) is less than water (1000 kg/m³)
questions.explanation: Less dense liquid floats on more dense liquid — immiscible layers separate by density

questions.question: Density 2.5 g/cm³, volume 8cm³. Mass =
What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
Archimedes' Principle:
Upthrust (buoyant force) = weight of fluid displaced
U = ρ_fluid × g × V_submerged

Law of Flotation:
Floating object: weight = upthrust
mg = ρ_fluid × g × V_displaced
∴ V_displaced/V_object = ρ_object/ρ_fluid

Apparent weight = actual weight - upthrust

content.title: Archimedes Examples
content.body:
Object weighs 50N in air. In water it weighs 32N.
Upthrust = 50-32 = 18N
Volume of water displaced = 18N/(1000×10) = 0.0018m³ = 1800cm³

Ship: steel is denser than water, but hollow hull displaces enough water to float (weight=upthrust)

content.title: Hydrometer
content.body:
Measures relative density of liquids
Floats lower in less dense liquid (sinks more, displaces more)
Floats higher in denser liquid (displaces less)
Used to check car battery acid, brewing beer, measuring milk quality

questions.question: Archimedes principle: upthrust equals
questions.explanation: Upthrust = weight of fluid DISPLACED by the submerged object

questions.question: Object weighs 30N in air, 22N in water. Upthrust =
questions.explanation: Upthrust = weight in air - weight in liquid = 30-22 = 8N

questions.question: A floating ship satisfies which condition
questions.explanation: For flotation: weight of ship = weight of water displaced (Archimedes)

questions.question: Steel is denser than water yet ships float because
options: hollow hull displaces water equal to ship's weight
questions.answer: hollow hull displaces water equal to ship's weight
questions.explanation: Large hollow hull → huge volume of water displaced → large upthrust = ship's total weight

questions.question: Apparent weight = actual weight MINUS upthrust. Object feels lighter in water because
options: upthrust acts upward, partially supporting the object
questions.answer: upthrust acts upward, partially supporting the object
questions.explanation: Upthrust reduces net downward force → object feels lighter in fluid

questions.question: Hydrometer sinks deeper in
questions.explanation: Less dense liquid: less upthrust per unit volume → hydrometer must displace more liquid → sinks deeper

questions.question: Object density = 600 kg/m³ floating in water (1000 kg/m³). Fraction submerged =
questions.explanation: V_submerged/V_total = ρ_object/ρ_fluid = 600/1000 = 0.6

questions.question: Submarines dive by
options: flooding ballast tanks to increase total density beyond seawater
questions.answer: flooding ballast tanks to increase total density beyond seawater
questions.explanation: Ballast tanks: fill with water → density exceeds seawater → net downward force → dive. Blow out water → surface

questions.question: Upthrust on fully submerged object in fresh water vs salt water
questions.explanation: Upthrust = ρ_fluid×g×V. Salt water is denser → greater upthrust for same submerged volume

questions.question: Hot air balloon rises because
options: hot air inside is less dense than surrounding cool air
questions.answer: hot air inside is less dense than surrounding cool air
questions.explanation: Heated air expands → less dense → balloon + air less dense than surrounding air → net upthrust > weight → rises

content.body:
Upthrust = Weight of fluid displaced
Upthrust = ρ_fluid × V_submerged × g

Archimedes' Principle:
A body immersed in fluid experiences upthrust equal to weight of fluid displaced

content.title: Worked: Steel in Water
content.body:
Steel block 200cm³ submerged in water (ρ=1000kg/m³)
Upthrust = 1000 × 0.0002 × 10 = 2 N
If block weighs 15N, apparent weight = 15-2 = 13N
An object floats when upthrust equals weight. If upthrust < weight, the object sinks. If upthrust > weight, the object rises until it floats partially submerged.

questions.question: Upthrust on a body equals
questions.explanation: Archimedes' Principle: upthrust = weight of fluid displaced by the body.

questions.question: A 500cm³ object submerged in water (ρ=1000kg/m³, g=10). Upthrust =
An object floats when
questions.explanation: Floating condition: weight exactly equals upthrust from displaced fluid.

questions.question: Apparent weight =
questions.explanation: When submerged, apparent weight = real weight minus upthrust.

questions.question: An iron ball weighs 10N in air and 7N in water. Upthrust =
questions.explanation: Upthrust = 10 − 7 = 3N

questions.question: Wood floats because
questions.explanation: Objects float when their average density is less than fluid density.

questions.question: Volume of water displaced by floating object equals
questions.explanation: Only the submerged portion displaces fluid.

questions.question: A ship floats in sea water (ρ=1025 kg/m³). If it moves to fresh water (ρ=1000), it
questions.explanation: Less dense fresh water gives less upthrust per volume, so ship sinks lower.

questions.question: Hydrometer measures
questions.explanation: Hydrometer floats at different levels in liquids of different density.

questions.question: Submarines control depth by
questions.explanation: Ballast tanks fill with water to sink or blow water out to rise.

content.body: Flotation principles are applied in many technologies: ships, submarines, hot air balloons, hydrometers, and life jackets. Understanding density and upthrust helps us design safer, more efficient vessels.

Relative Density (RD) = Density of substance / Density of water
RD = Weight in air / (Weight in air − Weight in water)
RD has no units

content.title: Relative Density Calculation
content.body:
Object weighs 50N in air, 30N in water.
RD = 50 / (50−30) = 50/20 = 2.5
Density = 2.5 × 1000 = 2500 kg/m³

questions.question: Relative density has
questions.explanation: Relative density is a ratio - no units.

questions.question: RD = Weight in air / Loss of weight in water. Loss of weight =
options: Weight in air − Weight in water
Weight in air + Weight in water
questions.answer: Weight in air − Weight in water
questions.explanation: Loss of weight = upthrust = W_air − W_water.

questions.question: Object: 80N in air, 50N in water. RD =
Hot air balloons rise because
questions.explanation: Heated air expands, becomes less dense than surrounding cool air, providing net upthrust.

questions.question: Life jackets work by
questions.explanation: Air-filled life jacket reduces wearer's average density below water density.

questions.question: Ships are made of steel yet float because
options: Ship hull shape traps air making average density < water
Sea water is denser than steel
questions.answer: Ship hull shape traps air making average density < water
questions.explanation: Hollow hull includes large air volume, reducing overall average density.

questions.question: RD of 0.8 means the substance
questions.explanation: RD 0.8 < 1 means density = 800 kg/m³ < 1000 kg/m³, so it floats.

questions.question: Plimsoll line on ships indicates
questions.explanation: Plimsoll line marks the maximum safe loading depth of a ship.

questions.question: Fish control buoyancy using
questions.explanation: Fish inflate/deflate their swim bladder to change average density and depth.

questions.question: Density of object = RD ×
questions.explanation: Density = RD × density of water = RD × 1000 kg/m³

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Density Flotation. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Density Flotation concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Density Flotation builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Density Flotation. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Density Flotation. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Density Flotation include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Density Flotation. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
